title: Bridging the Creativity Understanding Gap: Small-Scale Human Alignment Enables Expert-Level Humor Ranking in LLMs
research_questions: Can LLM humor ranking on the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest be decomposed into visual understanding, humor reasoning, and alignment with human crowd preferences?
Do improved cartoon descriptions and LLM-generated humor explanations improve pairwise humorous caption ranking?
Can persona-based prompting align LLM judgments with the New Yorker Caption Contest crowd, or is supervised fine-tuning on preference data required?
Can a preference-aligned LLM reach human expert-level accuracy on easy and harder New Yorker caption ranking tasks?
What do failures and successes in humor preference alignment imply for creative understanding and AGI?

datasets_used.name: New Yorker Caption Contest corpus
datasets_used.size: 679 contests selected from contests #1 to #763; 3 caption pairs per contest; human descriptions

datasets_used.name: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
datasets_used.size: 365 contests: #530 to #895; 6000+ ranked captions per contest; GPT-4o generated descriptions

datasets_used.name: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
datasets_used.size: A large corpus of New Yorker caption contest caption ranking data released by Zhang et al. (2024)

datasets_used.name: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset
datasets_used.size: 379 contests from contest #510 to #889; 20 constructed caption pairs per contest; 279 cartoons for training and 100 for testing

datasets_used.name: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset
datasets_used.size: 5,580 pairs of captions from 279 training cartoons

datasets_used.name: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset
datasets_used.size: 100 cartoons for testing; human expert evaluation used 50 contests selected from the testing set with two distinct caption pairs each

methods_proposed.name: Three-component humor ranking framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper decomposes humorous caption ranking into visual understanding, cartoon-caption humor reasoning, and alignment with human crowd preferences. The full pipeline uses refined cartoon descriptions, o1-preview-generated explanations, and preference-aligned ranking.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-4o-assisted human-in-the-loop description refinement
methods_proposed.summary: The authors assess GPT-4o-generated cartoon descriptions, identify inaccurate descriptions, iteratively correct them with human feedback, and generate both canny and uncanny descriptions for ranking experiments.

methods_proposed.name: Persona-based preference alignment prompts
methods_proposed.summary: The paper designs nine persona prompts intended to simulate New Yorker Caption Contest audience preferences, including lawyers, researchers, students, Bob Mankoff, Larry Wood, and cartoon authors, and tests them across four LLMs.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-4o supervised fine-tuning on crowd preferences with humor explanations
methods_proposed.summary: The authors fine-tune GPT-4o to choose the funnier caption from pairs using cartoon descriptions, two candidate captions, and o1-preview explanations, with labels derived from crowd rankings.

evaluation_methods.detail: Ground-truth caption rankings come from the New Yorker Caption Contest bandit-based crowdsource rating system, where users score captions as “funny”, “somewhat funny”, or “unfunny” and a complete crowdsourced ranking is obtained.
Models are evaluated by accuracy (%) on pairwise ranking tasks: ranks #1–10 versus #1000–1009 and ranks #30–39 versus #300–309.

Five renowned human experts in the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest world selected the funnier caption from pairs; the paper reports average accuracy, highest accuracy, majority accuracy, Fleiss Kappa, and agreement rate.
Human reviewers iteratively corrected inaccurate cartoon descriptions; the paper also states that a humor expert found over 85% of o1-preview explanations effectively captured a cartoon’s humor.

headline_results.finding: Quality assessment found that 23.5% of the machine-generated cartoon descriptions contained inaccuracies, corresponding to 89/379 descriptions.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

headline_results.finding: Using refined descriptions improved GPT-4o prompting accuracy from 70% to 73%, and improved finetuned model performance from 81.3% to 82.4%.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

headline_results.finding: For top 10 versus 1000–1009 caption ranking, o1-preview explanations gave 76% GPT-4o accuracy, compared with 73% for no explanations and 71% for GPT-4o explanations.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

headline_results.finding: Persona-based prompting produced only modest gains: the best GPT-4o persona was Female Lawyer at 76.5%, versus 73.5 for the empty baseline on the size 200 subset.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

headline_results.finding: On 10vs1000, GPT-4o SFT w/ Expl. achieved 82.4_{\pm1.2}, outperforming GPT-4o Prompting at 67.3_{\pm1.5} and Expert Average Accuracy at 78_{\pm2.6}, while below Best Expert Accuracy at 85.33_{\pm2.9}.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

headline_results.finding: On the harder 30vs300 task, GPT-4o SFT w/ Expl. achieved 63.2_{\pm1.5}, compared with GPT-4o Prompting at 53.9_{\pm1.6}, Expert Average Accuracy at 61.6_{\pm3.0}, Expert Majority Vote at 66_{\pm6.8}, and Best Expert Accuracy at 68_{\pm3.8}.
headline_results.dataset: Refined New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest caption-pair dataset

key_findings: The authors identify visual description quality as a real bottleneck: 23.5% (89/379) of GPT-4o-generated cartoon descriptions had errors, and correcting them improved downstream ranking accuracy.
o1-preview explanations improved caption ranking accuracy to 76% in Table 1, while GPT-4o-generated explanations reduced it to 71%, showing that explanation quality matters.
Persona-based prompting was weak for preference alignment: the best GPT-4o prompt reached 76.5% on a size 200 subset, only 3% above the 73.5 baseline without a persona.
Supervised fine-tuning on 5,580 crowd preference pairs substantially improved GPT-4o, reaching 82.4_{\pm1.2} on 10vs1000 and 63.2_{\pm1.5} on 30vs300 when using o1-preview explanations.
The fine-tuned model exceeded expert average accuracy on both reported tasks but still slightly underperformed the best individual expert on the 10vs1000 task.
limitations: The study is based solely on the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest dataset, which may limit generalization to other humor forms and creative tasks.
The paper primarily evaluates caption understanding through pairwise ranking, which may not capture broader humor understanding or humor generation challenges.
The preference data are subjective and reflect the tastes of a specific audience, such as New Yorker readers.
Collecting high-quality curated human preference data is resource-intensive and may not scale easily to other creative domains.
The framework does not explicitly address detection or mitigation of offensive, culturally insensitive, or derogatory humorous content.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper warns that it contains potentially offensive content due to the nature of humor, and its limitations state that humorous content can be culturally insensitive or derogatory and that the current framework does not explicitly mitigate offensive content. Appendix D states the work is for research purposes and complies with CC-BY-4.0 and CC-BY-NC-4.0 dataset licenses.

artifacts.other: No code or data URL is stated. Appendix D states that the experiments used OpenAI, Anthropic and Google APIs, cost around $4,000 USD, and that LLMs were used to rephrase some parts of the paper.

human_samples.population: New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest entrants submitting captions
human_samples.recruitment: Weekly public contest invites readers to submit humorous captions for a captionless cartoon.
human_samples.observations: The paper states that each week over 6,000 captions are submitted; the study uses human-submitted captions from NYCCC contests, especially contests #510–#889 / #530–#895 depending on dataset source.
human_samples.detail: These are caption observations, not participant counts. The paper does not report the number of distinct caption submitters.

human_samples.population: Users/readers participating in the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest crowd voting/rating system
human_samples.recruitment: Crowdsource voting page for the Caption Contest; users score captions as “funny,” “somewhat funny,” or “unfunny.”
human_samples.observations: For contests #530 to #895, the bandit-based rating system yields complete crowdsourced caption rankings. Zhang et al. (2024) is described as having 6,000+ ranked captions per contest.
human_samples.detail: These crowd rankings provide the ground-truth preference labels for model training/evaluation. The paper does not report the number of distinct voters or total votes/ratings.

human_samples.population: Prior human annotators from the Hessel et al. (2023) annotated NYCCC dataset
human_samples.observations: A subset of the 379 cartoons came from the annotated dataset introduced by Hessel et al. (2023), which had human descriptions.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses these prior human annotations/descriptions for some cartoons but does not specify how many cartoons in the subset or how many annotators.

human_samples.population: Human reviewers for cartoon-description quality assessment and refinement
human_samples.observations: Quality assessment covered 379 GPT-4o-generated cartoon descriptions; 23.5% (89/379) were identified as inaccurate and then iteratively corrected or regenerated with human feedback.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes “human reviewers” but does not report their number, qualifications, blinding, or exact review protocol.

human_samples.observations: The paper states that the humor expert found that over 85% of o1-preview explanations effectively captured a cartoon’s humor.
human_samples.detail: The number of explanations reviewed, selection procedure, criteria, and blinding are not reported.

human_samples.population: Highly renowned human experts in the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest world, including famous cartoonists, editors, and podcast hosts; Bob Mankoff is specifically mentioned as the best-performing individual expert.
human_samples.recruitment: Authors conducted a study with these experts; the paper notes that due to busy schedules the evaluation corpus was limited to 50 contests from the testing set.
human_samples.observations: Each expert completed 100 trials: 50 contests × two caption-pair comparisons, one rank-1 vs rank-1000 pair and one rank-30 vs rank-300 pair per contest. Total expert judgments: 5 experts × 100 trials, excluding attention checks.
human_samples.detail: Appendix C reports average accuracy, highest accuracy, majority accuracy, Fleiss kappa, and agreement rate. The paper does not report compensation, demographics beyond professional expertise, or whether experts were blinded to caption ranks.

judges.name: New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest crowdsource rating system / crowd voters
judges.detail: Crowd scores of captions as “funny,” “somewhat funny,” or “unfunny” are converted into complete rankings and used as the ground-truth labels for pairwise accuracy and for SFT preference data.

judges.name: Pairwise ranking accuracy
judges.detail: Model or human choices between two captions are scored as correct when they select the caption with the better crowd ranking. Reported tasks include #1–10 vs #1000–1009 / rank-1 vs rank-1000 and #30–39 vs #300–309 / rank-30 vs rank-300 variants.

judges.name: GPT-4o, GPT-4o SFT variants, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking / Thinking Experiment, o1, and o3-mini
judges.detail: These LLM systems act as caption rankers/verifiers by choosing the funnier of two candidate captions; their selections determine the model-performance results that are scored against crowd rankings.

judges.name: Five NYCCC-domain experts
judges.detail: Experts selected the funnier caption from pairs and determine the human expert average, majority-vote, highest-accuracy, and agreement results in Tables 3 and 5.

judges.name: Human reviewers for visual-description refinement
judges.detail: Reviewers identified and corrected erroneous or incomplete GPT-4o cartoon descriptions; the paper does not state whether they were lay or expert, so expertise is not assumed.

judges.name: Humor expert evaluating o1-preview explanations
judges.detail: A humor expert judged that over 85% of o1-preview explanations effectively captured the cartoon’s humor; this supports the use of o1-preview explanations but is not the primary accuracy metric.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4o cartoon-description generation and human-in-the-loop refinement
generation_selection.detail: GPT-4o generated descriptions for cartoons lacking human annotations and regenerated problematic descriptions after specific human feedback until descriptions met accuracy/completeness criteria. The number of generations per image and any alternative-candidate selection procedure are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: Uncanny-description generation from validated canny descriptions
generation_selection.n: one response requested per prompt; no alternatives reported
generation_selection.detail: After clean canny descriptions were established, the authors used a 5-shot prompt to ask GPT-4o to write an uncanny description. No best-of-N or candidate selection is described.

generation_selection.scope: Humor-explanation generation with GPT-4o and o1-preview
generation_selection.n: one explanation response per caption is described; no alternatives reported
generation_selection.detail: Appendix A.2 uses a zero-shot prompt with canny and uncanny descriptions plus the caption, asking for bullet-point reasons why the caption is funny. No best-of-N selection is reported.

generation_selection.n: one A/B answer per caption pair
generation_selection.detail: Baseline and persona/ICL ranking prompts ask the model to choose either A or B and nothing else. Repeated sampling, self-consistency, majority voting, or best-of-N output selection are not reported.

generation_selection.n: one choice between two candidate captions per pair
generation_selection.detail: The SFT models are trained to choose between two candidate captions given the cartoon description and, for the explanation variant, o1-preview explanations. No best-of-N inference is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used for cartoon descriptions, baseline/prompted caption ranking, GPT-4o-generated explanations, and as the base model for fine-tuning. Exact OpenAI API snapshot/version and inference dates are not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o SFT w/o Expl. (Ours)
model_provenance.detail: Supervised fine-tuned GPT-4o variant trained on pairwise caption preferences without explanation inputs. Base snapshot, fine-tuning job details, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o SFT w/ Expl. (Ours)
model_provenance.detail: Supervised fine-tuned GPT-4o variant trained/evaluated with o1-preview-generated explanation inputs. Base snapshot, fine-tuning job details, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: o1-preview
model_provenance.detail: Used to generate humor explanations that are supplied to rankers and SFT models. Exact OpenAI snapshot and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: o1
model_provenance.detail: Used as a prompted caption-ranking baseline in Table 3. The paper distinguishes Table 3’s “o1” from o1-preview explanation generation but gives no exact snapshot.

model_provenance.name: o3-mini
model_provenance.detail: Used in persona-prompting experiments and Table 3 prompted caption-ranking results. Exact OpenAI snapshot and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet / Claude-3.5-sonnet
model_provenance.detail: Used in persona-prompting experiments and Table 3 prompted caption-ranking results. Exact Anthropic snapshot and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking / Gemini-2.0-Thinking-Experiment
model_provenance.detail: Used in persona-prompting experiments and Table 3 prompted caption-ranking results. Table 2 calls it “Gemini-2.0-Thinking-Experiment,” while Table 3 calls it “Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking.” Exact Google API snapshot and inference date are not reported.

inference_budgets.detail: Appendix D states that OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google APIs were used for all experiments and that the overall experiments cost around $4,000 USD. Per-model or per-experiment costs are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Dataset split and pairwise-ranking item construction
inference_budgets.detail: The study uses 379 contests split randomly into 279 training cartoons and 100 testing cartoons. For SFT training, each training cartoon contributes 10 pairs from ranks #1–10 vs #1000–1009 and 10 pairs from #30–39 vs #300–309, totaling 5,580 pairs. Table 2 persona experiments are measured on a size-200 subset of the test set.

Baseline ranking uses a 5-shot prompt with canny and uncanny descriptions and asks for A/B. Persona/explanation variants add persona system prompts and o1-preview explanations. Number of API calls, retries, seeds, and decoding settings are not reported.

Nine persona prompts plus an empty baseline are evaluated across GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, and o3-mini on a size-200 subset; all persona experiments incorporate five random in-context examples and o1-preview explanations. Token limits and sampling parameters are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: one caption is explained per prompt; no alternative explanations reported
inference_budgets.detail: GPT-4o and o1-preview generate explanations zero-shot from canny/uncanny descriptions and a caption. The number of generated explanations, calls, retries, and decoding settings are not reported.

GPT-4o is used to generate descriptions for 379 images and to regenerate problematic descriptions after human feedback; uncanny descriptions are generated with a 5-shot prompt. The number of iterations/calls per image and sampling parameters are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4o supervised fine-tuning and SFT inference
inference_budgets.detail: SFT uses 5,580 training pairs from 279 cartoons. Inputs include cartoon descriptions, two candidate captions, and for the explanation variant, corresponding o1-preview explanations. Fine-tuning hyperparameters, epochs, batch size, learning rate, and fine-tuning compute are not reported.

human_baselines.description: Five human experts selected the funnier caption from 100 trials each; reported metrics include expert majority vote, expert average accuracy, best expert accuracy, Fleiss kappa, and agreement rate.
human_baselines.detail: The baseline is not fully matched to the model evaluations: experts saw the original cartoon image, while models generally saw textual descriptions and often o1-preview explanations; experts evaluated only 50 contests from the test set with one rank-1 vs rank-1000 and one rank-30 vs rank-300 pair per contest, while model evaluations are reported for broader test-set/tier tasks.

human_baselines.scope: Non-expert human baseline under matched prompt conditions
human_baselines.detail: The paper does not report a lay-human baseline completing the same description-plus-explanation A/B task as the models.

mitigations: The paper uses a random train/test split with 279 training cartoons and 100 testing cartoons, so SFT training pairs are separated from the authors’ test cartoons.
Human expert evaluation uses contests selected from the testing set.
contamination_risk.basis: The underlying NYCCC cartoons, captions, and crowd rankings come from public contests and prior datasets/benchmarks such as Hessel et al. (2023) and Zhang et al. (2024), and several evaluated proprietary models postdate at least some of those sources. The exact refined split/pairs may be newly constructed, but they are derived from public source material and public ranking data. The paper does not report any pretraining-contamination analysis, API provider decontamination guarantees, held-out/private item construction, or memorization tests; the random train/test split only addresses leakage from the authors’ SFT training, not contamination of base proprietary models.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Claims about LLM caption-ranking/verifier performance, including expert-level GPT-4o SFT accuracy and the usefulness of o1-preview explanations, depend on LLMs selecting the funnier caption and generating intermediate descriptions/explanations.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Correctness is anchored to NYCCC crowd rankings; five human experts provide a baseline on a test subset; human reviewers validate/correct descriptions; one humor expert validates o1-preview explanation quality above 85%.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLM is reported as the ground-truth labeler or as an external grader of another model’s generated humor. However, the evaluated systems themselves are LLM judges/rankers of caption funniness, and o1-preview-generated explanations are a central input to major model results.

reporting_gaps: Exact API model snapshots/versions and inference dates are not reported for GPT-4o, o1-preview, o1, o3-mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, or Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking.
Decoding/sampling parameters, temperature, seeds, retry policy, and token limits are not reported for ranking, description generation, or explanation generation.
The number of API calls per item and per experiment is not reported; only the approximate total experiment cost of $4,000 USD is given.
The number of distinct NYCCC crowd voters, total ratings/votes, voter demographics, and vote aggregation details beyond the bandit-based ranking system are not reported.
The number, qualifications, recruitment, compensation, and blinding of human reviewers who refined cartoon descriptions are not reported.
The protocol for the description quality assessment is underspecified: criteria, adjudication, number of reviewers per description, and inter-reviewer agreement are not reported.
The humor-expert validation of o1-preview explanations does not report the sample size, sampling method, rubric, or blinding.
Fine-tuning hyperparameters and OpenAI fine-tuning job details, including epochs, batch size, learning rate, validation procedure, and base snapshot, are not reported.
The random train/test split seed and full list of train/test contest IDs or exact evaluation pairs are not provided in the paper text.
Human expert study details such as compensation, demographics beyond expertise, geography, recruitment procedure, and whether experts were blinded to caption ranks are not reported.
Model scores on the exact same 100 expert-evaluation trials are not separately reported, limiting direct human-model comparability.

Table 3 states that prompted models use the best persona, but the paper does not fully specify a separate validation procedure for choosing personas; Table 2 persona results are measured on a test subset.
No data or code release URL is stated in the paper text.
The paper does not discuss pretraining contamination or perform a decontamination/memorization test for public NYCCC captions and rankings.
